entities.name: unit
entities.cite: Take the units — phoneme, word, sentence

entities.name: bhāga
entities.cite: Either they have parts or they do not

entities.name: abhyupāya
entities.cite: a device, the analyst's and the hearer's working fiction

axioms.name: arises_from
axioms.cite: the ordering belongs to the manifesting stream, projected onto the unit

axioms.name: is_abhyupaya
axioms.cite: the very positing of parts, is abhyupaya — a device, the analyst's and the hearer's working fiction

denials.name: inherent_to
denials.cite: no real internal sequence belongs to the linguistic unit itself

claims.name: arises_from
claims.cite: the difference of form arises from the sequence of the sound

claims.name: is_abhyupaya
claims.cite: the construing of a difference of parts is a device

rejected_readings.label: intrinsic_divisibility
rejected_readings.rendering: Linguistic units possess real internal parts arranged in sequence.
rejected_readings.why: Ignores the dilemma: both horns deny intrinsic divisibility. The first horn locates sequence in the manifesting stream (not the unit); the second denies that parts belong to the unit at all—they are posited as a device. This reading mistakes the analyst's projection for the unit's actual structure.

rejected_readings.label: asymmetric_dilemma
rejected_readings.rendering: Only the partless horn (nirbhaga) resolves the part-whole paradox; the parted horn (bhagavat) leaves real divisibility intact.
rejected_readings.why: Misses that Bhartrhari uses both horns symmetrically: each denies real internal sequence to the unit. The first locates it in the stream; the second demotes it to device. Neither concedes intrinsic divisibility. A reading that privileges one horn over the other fractures the solution.

notes: The contract captures Bhartrhari's resolution of the part-whole paradox: no real sequence belongs to the unit itself; apparent sequences are either manifestations in the phonetic stream (bhāgavat horn) or the analyst's device (nirbhāga horn). The six sorts fit: absolute is absent (no ultimate here), but power (abhyupaya), manifestation (dhvani), linguisticItem (unit), and property (bhāga, rūpabheda, krama) all appear naturally. The dilemma's two-horned structure is encoded in two separate axioms addressing each case.
